claim: Per-section Zipf exponents vary monotonically with section entropy: the text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits) has a Zipf exponent closest to 1.0, the zodiac section (entropy 3.7149 bits) has the flattest Zipf exponent (furthest below 1.0), and all other sections fall in betweenâ€”consistent with the text-only section being least transformed from natural language and label-heavy sections being most compressed or formulaic.
evidence_for: Overall Zipf exponent 0.8946 is below natural language baseline of ~1.0, indicating artificial compression or label-repetition effects
Zodiac entropy 3.7149 (lowest) is consistent with a small repeated label vocabulary, which produces flat Zipf distributions
Text-only section entropy 3.9016 (highest) is most similar to natural language entropy (Latin ~4.0, Italian ~3.95)
The 0.987 confidence belief that text-only section represents the closest approximation to natural language directly predicts this
evidence_against: Small section sizes (zodiac: 1,297 words; text-only: 2,349 words) make Zipf fits noisy and confidence intervals wide
If zodiac pages use a fixed label vocabulary rather than running text, the low entropy reflects vocabulary size not cipher depth, and the relationship to Zipf may be non-monotonic
test: Fit Zipf power law (log-log OLS) to word frequency distribution for each of the 7 sections separately. Record exponent and R-squared. Compute Spearman rank correlation between section entropy and section Zipf exponent across all 7 sections. Bootstrap 1,000 random section-size-matched samples from the full corpus to establish null correlation distribution.
test_metric: Spearman rho between section entropy and section Zipf exponent; individual section exponents for zodiac and text-only
test_threshold: Confirm if Spearman rho > 0.7 AND text-only Zipf exponent > 0.92 AND zodiac Zipf exponent < 0.80; deny if rho < 0.4 or ordering is violated for text-only/zodiac pair
tests_remaining: Fit Zipf power law (log-log OLS) to word frequency distribution for each of the 7 sections separately. Record exponent and R-squared. Compute Spearman rank correlation between section entropy and section Zipf exponent across all 7 sections. Bootstrap 1,000 random section-size-matched samples from the full corpus to establish null correlation distribution.
reasoning: A monotonic entropy-Zipf relationship across sections would unify multiple high-confidence beliefs into a single testable prediction and identify which sections are most and least transformed from natural languageâ€”directly prioritizing which sections to attempt decipherment on first
